text:
Andy Murraywon everything possible during his glittering career, claiming 46 titles - including three Grand Slams, two Olympic gold medals, and 14 Masters 1000s. The Brit also reached the top of the rankings and received the year-end No. 1 trophy in 2016. Now that he’s been retired for more than a year, some would assume that Murray’s silverware has extra meaning to him - but the 38-year-old has revealed that he’s no longer as protective of his Olympic medals, even letting his children “scratch” them.

Murray’s trophies still have their own designated spot in his home - courtesy of his wife, Kim’s handiwork. But the two-timeWimbledonchampion isn’t fussed about spending time with his hard-earned cups.

“When we moved house my wife made like a trophy cabinet. It’s downstairs, out the way but yeah, there’s one in there. It is pretty big, it’s a decent size. But actually, I mean, I’m fine with them being in there, but I don’t go and look at them daily. It’s not something that I think about loads,” the former world No. 1 told the Tennis Podcast.

Although Murray isn’t enamoured by his trophies, he used to treat his two Olympic gold medals like a prized possession. One had been on display at the Wimbledon museum until very recently. But nowadays, the Brit is a little more careless with his London and Rio golds - and his four children have more fun with them than he does.

He continued: “I actually got it back last week. It had been in there for, I think, about a year, but yeah, I got it back recently. Olympic medals, yeah, they’re in there [the cabinet]. Do I ever wear them? My kids wear them sometimes, yeah.

“I was quite precious about them in the beginning, but now I’m like, ‘Whatever, it’s fine. Scratch them up and stuff, whatever’. One of them wanted to take one of them into school, I don’t know what it was. They were doing something at school and we were like, no, that’s a bit much to send them in with one of them. So we gave them my Beijing Olympics participation medal instead, which they were happy with.”



Murray might not be so careful with his medals, but the three-time Major winner has some other important Olympics mementos in his home - the famous Olympic pins. During his farewell tournament at the Paris 2024 Games, the Brit went out of his way to collect as many rare pins as possible. However, he’s now been left wondering whether it was worth it.

“I’ve still got all of them, yeah. I’ve kept all of them. That was something that, at the time, I absolutely loved doing it every single Olympics whereas now I’m a bit like, did I sort of spend a bit too much time walking around the village and stopping at different tables in the restaurant?” he laughed.

“Just like, oh, that guy is from Eritrea, that’s a rare pin. I’ll go over and ask if he’s got one, and they’re just, like, having scrambled eggs.”

title: Murray reveals where he keeps trophies but doesn't care about Olympic medals | Tennis | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Yasmin Syed
description: Andy Murray was once very precious with his Olympic gold medals - but not anymore.
